content.title: Events System
content.description:
Hobson includes a type-hierarchy-aware event system built into [kernel:core](item://33333333-1111-0000-0000-000000000000). The [`EventBus`](item://33333333-1111-0000-0000-000000000000#code?region=EventBus) class enables decoupled communication between items—data changes, system events, and errors all flow through the event bus.

---

## Event Structure

Events are objects with type and content:

```javascript
{
  type: "e0e00000-0001-0002-0000-000000000000",  // Event type GUID
  content: { id: "...", item: {...} },           // Event-specific payload
  timestamp: 1738368000000                       // Auto-added if missing
}
```

---

## Event Type Hierarchy

Event types form a type chain like items. Subscribing to a parent type receives all child events.

```
event-definition (base)                  e0e00000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000
├── item-event                           e0e00000-0001-0000-0000-000000000000
│   ├── item:created                     e0e00000-0001-0001-0000-000000000000
│   ├── item:updated                     e0e00000-0001-0002-0000-000000000000
│   └── item:deleted                     e0e00000-0001-0003-0000-000000000000
└── system-event                         e0e00000-0002-0000-0000-000000000000
    ├── system:error                     e0e00000-0002-0001-0000-000000000000
    └── system:boot-complete             e0e00000-0002-0002-0000-000000000000
```

### Type Hierarchy Dispatch

When an event is emitted, the system:
1. Looks up the event type's full ancestor chain
2. Notifies all listeners subscribed to any type in that chain

```javascript
// Subscribe to ALL item events (created, updated, deleted)
api.events.on(api.EVENT_IDS.ITEM_EVENT, (event) => {
  console.log('Item event:', event.type, event.content);
});

// Subscribe only to item:deleted
api.events.on(api.EVENT_IDS.ITEM_DELETED, (event) => {
  console.log('Item deleted:', event.content.id);
});
```

---

## Programmatic API

### `api.events.on(eventTypeId, handler)`

Subscribe to an event type. Returns unsubscribe function.

```javascript
const unsubscribe = api.events.on(api.EVENT_IDS.ITEM_UPDATED, (event) => {
  console.log('Updated:', event.content.item.name);
});

// Later:
unsubscribe();
```

### `api.events.off(eventTypeId, handler)`

Remove a specific handler.

### `api.events.emit(event)`

Emit an event. Typically only kernel code emits core events, but custom events can be defined.

```javascript
api.events.emit({
  type: 'my-custom-event-uuid',
  content: { message: 'Hello!' }
});
```

### `api.events.list()`

Returns array of event type IDs with active listeners.

---

## Built-in Events

### Item Events

| Event | ID Constant | Content |
|-------|-------------|----------|
| `item:created` | `ITEM_CREATED` | `{ id, item }` |
| `item:updated` | `ITEM_UPDATED` | `{ id, item, previous }` |
| `item:deleted` | `ITEM_DELETED` | `{ id, item }` |

### System Events

| Event | ID Constant | Content |
|-------|-------------|----------|
| `system:error` | `SYSTEM_ERROR` | `{ error: {name, message, stack}, context: {...} }` |
| `system:boot-complete` | `SYSTEM_BOOT_COMPLETE` | `{ safeMode, debugMode, lateActivation }` |

---

## Declarative Watches

Code items can declare event watches in their `content.watches` array. See [`declarativeWatches`](item://33333333-1111-0000-0000-000000000000#code?region=declarativeWatches) for the implementation. This enables libraries to activate on boot or react to item changes without explicit subscription code.

### Watch Declaration

```javascript
{
  id: "...",
  type: api.IDS.LIBRARY,
  content: {
    watches: [
      { event: "e0e00000-0002-0002-0000-000000000000" },  // system:boot-complete
      { event: "e0e00000-0001-0002-0000-000000000000", type: "my-type-uuid" }  // item:updated for specific type
    ],
    code: `
      export function onSystemBootComplete(content, api) {
        console.log('Library activated!');
      }
      
      export function onItemUpdated(content, api) {
        console.log('Item updated:', content.item.name);
      }
    `
  }
}
```

### Watch Filters

Watches can filter by:

| Filter | Effect |
|--------|--------|
| `event` | Event type GUID to watch (required) |
| `type` | Only match if affected item has this exact type |
| `typeExtends` | Only match if affected item's type chain includes this type |
| `id` | Only match if affected item has this specific ID |

### Handler Naming Convention

Event names map to handler function names:

| Event Name | Handler Name |
|------------|---------------|
| `item:created` | `onItemCreated` |
| `item:updated` | `onItemUpdated` |
| `item:deleted` | `onItemDeleted` |
| `system:error` | `onSystemError` |
| `system:boot-complete` | `onSystemBootComplete` |

### Handler Signature

```javascript
export async function onEventName(content, api) {
  // content = event.content (not full event object)
  // api = full REPL API
}
```

---

## Late Activation

When a code item with `system:boot-complete` watch is saved, the kernel immediately calls its handler with `lateActivation: true`. This enables:

- Hot-loading new libraries without reload
- Testing event handlers by saving the code item
- Gradual system extension

---

## Error Events

The kernel captures errors via [`captureError()`](item://33333333-1111-0000-0000-000000000000#code?region=errorCapture) and emits `system:error` events:

```javascript
// In kernel code:
await kernel.captureError(error, {
  operation: 'render',
  itemId: item.id,
  itemName: item.name
});
```

### Error Handling Flow

1. Error occurs in rendering or other operation
2. `captureError()` emits `system:error` event
3. Libraries watching `system:error` can log, display, or recover
4. If no handlers, kernel shows fallback error banner

### Fallback Error UI

When no `system:error` handlers exist (or all fail), the kernel displays a red banner at the top of the viewport with the error message and a dismiss button.

---

## Event Type Cache

At boot, the kernel builds a cache mapping each event type ID to:
- **ancestors**: Set of all type IDs in the chain (for hierarchy dispatch)
- **name**: The event's name string (for handler name derivation)

This enables O(1) lookups during event dispatch.

---

## Custom Event Types

Create custom event types by extending the event hierarchy:

```javascript
// Create a custom event type
await api.set({
  id: crypto.randomUUID(),
  name: 'my-app:data-synced',
  type: api.EVENT_IDS.SYSTEM_EVENT,  // Extends system-event
  content: {
    description: 'Emitted when data sync completes'
  }
});

// Rebuild event cache to recognize new type
await kernel.buildEventTypeCache();
```

Note: Custom event types require a kernel cache rebuild to participate in hierarchy dispatch.
